title: Forced Google DNS fallback in rootless Podman can cause DNS leaks and bypass local resolvers
body:
**The following scenario can occur when using:**

Podman rootless with [Pasta](https://passt.top/passt/about/) and [aardvark-dns](https://github.com/containers/aardvark-dns) . This is the default scenario when creating a Podman network with the parameters: `driver=bridge`, `internal=false`, and `disable-dns=false`. 

> Specifically, I am using [quadlet](https://docs.podman.io/en/latest/markdown/podman-systemd.unit.5.html) on [NixOS](https://nixos.org/) via [quadlet-nix](https://github.com/SEIAROTg/quadlet-nix) (in rootless mode), but the description of the issue below should also apply to other setups.

**The problem:**

In certain scenarios, [resolveconf.go](https://github.com/containers/common/blob/main/libnetwork/resolvconf/resolvconf.go#L21) hardcodes Google DNS servers `8.8.8.8` and `8.8.4.4` (and their IPv6 counterparts) in the `resolv.conf` generated inside the _rootless netns_ namespace. Google DNS should not be forced, as there are scenarios where avoiding DNS leaks is essential. There is no option in [containers.conf](https://github.com/containers/common/blob/main/docs/containers.conf.5.md), [Pasta](https://passt.top/builds/latest/web/passt.1.html), or [aardvark-dns](https://github.com/containers/aardvark-dns) that can prevent this.

**How does this DNS leak scenario occur?**

1. Podman is being used as initially described.
2. A local DNS server (e.g., [Unbound](https://www.nlnetlabs.nl/projects/unbound/about/) or [dnscrypt-proxy2](https://github.com/DNSCrypt/dnscrypt-proxy)) is running on the **host** at `127.0.0.1`. For this to work properly, the host's `/etc/resolv.conf` must contain nameserver `127.0.0.1` so that the local resolver is used. Note: We want all host services, as well as all containers, to use this local resolution service exclusively. We do not want any fallback to public DNS servers for security reasons.
3. When Podman creates the _rootless netns_ namespace, it [copies the contents](https://github.com/containers/common/blob/main/libnetwork/resolvconf/resolv.go#L63) of host `resolv.conf` into the namespace's `resolv.conf`. During this copy process [this function](https://github.com/containers/common/blob/main/libnetwork/resolvconf/resolvconf.go#L49) will only detect `127.0.0.1` (as `resolvConf`) and **forcibly** append Google's DNS servers.
4. Pasta will **later** ([1](https://github.com/containers/common/blob/main/libnetwork/internal/rootlessnetns/netns_linux.go#L231) [2](https://github.com/containers/common/blob/main/libnetwork/resolvconf/resolv.go#L147)) add the host-mapped IP to the namespace's `resolv.conf`. This is intended to properly link resolution between the rootless namespace and the host. But despite this, Google's nameservers will still remain in the `resolv.conf`. No Pasta option that can be included as [pasta_options in containers.conf](https://github.com/containers/common/blob/main/docs/containers.conf.5.md#network-table) can change this behavior.
5. Aardvark-dns uses the `resolv.conf` (of  _rootless netns_) file as a fallback/upstream resolver when it's not trying to resolve a container name as a domain. [This behavior](https://github.com/containers/aardvark-dns/blob/d550720140aa6e865483d595fb3770d85709b9d8/src/server/serve.rs#L369) cannot be changed using any aardvark-dns option.
6. In a scenario where the host's local DNS server stops working, aardvark-dns will fall back to using Google DNS for container resolution. This can cause a DNS leak, exposing (unencrypted) the domains accessed by the containers. This will happen even if the **host** `resolv.conf` only has nameserver `127.0.0.1`, and even if services like Unbound or dnscrypt-proxy2 are configured to reject all resolutions in case of failure.

**Possible solution**

Allow the value of `defaultIPv4Dns`/`defaultIPv6Dns` in [resolvconf.go](https://github.com/containers/common/blob/main/libnetwork/resolvconf/resolvconf.go#L21) to be defined via containers.conf, and allow the value "null" to indicate that **no** fallback DNS servers should be added to the `resolv.conf` inside the rootless namespace. This way, it would be possible to handle the scenario where we only want our containers to use the local DNS server of our host.

Another option could be to allow changing the value of [KeepHostServers](https://github.com/containers/common/blob/main/libnetwork/internal/rootlessnetns/netns_linux.go#L238) through a containers.conf setting to avoid [this](https://github.com/containers/common/blob/main/libnetwork/resolvconf/resolv.go#L149).

Note:
There are some ways to use Podman and/or Pasta DNS options to avoid the problem. However, in all of these "solutions", aardvark-dns can no longer be used, and the valuable functionality of resolving container names as domain names for inter-container communication is lost. I believe the underlying issue is the forced use of Google DNS servers when the criteria I’ve outlined are met.

comments.body:
I never liked the google fall back behavior but it is something that we inherited from docker and I am not sure we cn chnage that anytime soon.

As for specifying custom servers does dns_servers in containers.conf not work? They should definitely be taken over the google ones I think.

Yes, when using `dns_servers` from `containers.conf`, the `resolv.conf` file inside the containers is overwritten. However, by doing this, _aardvark-dns_ can no longer be used for inter-container resolution.

The problem lies with the `resolv.conf` generated in the _rootless network namespace_. This resolver, used by _aardvark-dns_, cannot be externally modified and always has Google's DNS servers "stuck" to it.

If you’d like, I can provide a detailed breakdown of how each resolv.conf is affected depending on the options used.

My concern is that many users might be relying on their host `resolv.conf` without knowing that Google DNS servers are embedded in an intermediate layer. This can be especially critical if host-level VPNs are used to control traffic.

The only way to become aware of what I’ve described is by sniffing the network or manually restart _aardvark-dns_ in debug mode and checking the logs. There are no warnings or documentation related to this issue.

> Yes, when using dns_servers from containers.conf, the resolv.conf file inside the containers is overwritten. However, by doing this, aardvark-dns can no longer be used for inter-container resolution.

That should not be the case on any recent podman, when using aardvark-dns the nameservers should be send to aardvark-dns instead and aardvark-dns itself will forward accordingly and then the container resolv.conf still only has the aardvark-dns ip in it
You can also set nameservers for the entire network with `podman network create --dns` which works similar. In such cases aardvark-dns should not forward to the resolvers from the resolv.conf in the rootless-netns file.


> The problem lies with the resolv.conf generated in the rootless network namespace. This resolver, used by aardvark-dns, cannot be externally modified and always has Google's DNS servers "stuck" to it.

I think one issue is that this seems to ignore the dns_servers option. That is definitely something we should fix.



---

Note the reason this behaviour exists in the first place is because by default as root (no aardvark-dns involved) there is no way to reach localhost resolvers on the host form the container. As such a fall back is provided because for most folks having dns out of the box is better than no dns at all without manual config.


---

The other case as you said is we already have the pasta forwarder ip do we really need to add the fall back servers then? I think it comes down to the same issue. Because if the first resolver that pasta is using goes down then the whole dns is broken. Having a fall back is desirable for many people, at least the ones not caring about a dns leak.
If you must block leaks presumably you already should have host firewall rules blocking that traffic? Because any application can choose to bypass the resolv.conf server for any reason anyway. 

---

> The only way to become aware of what I’ve described is by sniffing the network or manually restart aardvark-dns in debug mode and checking the logs. There are no warnings or documentation related to this issue.

Documentation patches are always welcome.

This issue is broader than just fallback to the Google public DNS. Podman extracts public nameservers from systemd-resolved and NetworkManager configs. This results in aardvark-dns sending unencrypted requests to the servers configured in the local resolver if that resolver was too slow.

> You can also set nameservers for the entire network with podman network create --dns which works similar. In such cases aardvark-dns should not forward to the resolvers from the resolv.conf in the rootless-netns file.

Passing `--dns=169.254.1.1` to `podman network create` solved the issue for me. The special address `169.254.1.1` used by pasta to forward dns requests to the host. In quadlet syntax this would be written as:
```
[Network]
DNS=169.254.1.1
```
Don't forget to delete the old network, as network quadlet cannot change the configuration of an existing network.

---

This is a summary of the conversation with @Luap99 here: https://github.com/containers/aardvark-dns/discussions/626

@magras 
Thanks for the input. Personally, I ended up blocking any outgoing request to 8.8.8.8 or 8.8.4.4 on my host.

Regarding what you mentioned — unfortunately, I don’t have much time to test right now, but please verify this. Use this command to read the contents of the resolv.conf file inside the rootless namespace. This resolv.conf will be used by both pasta and aardvark-dns.

`podman unshare --rootless-netns /bin/sh -c 'while read line; do echo "$line"; done < /etc/resolv.conf'`

Ideally, it should only contain the IP you mentioned — that is, your host’s IP and nothing else. If other entries are listed, and your resolver on the host fails or takes too long, the requests will be sent using those fallbacks.

> Ideally, it should only contain the IP you mentioned — that is, your host’s IP and nothing else. If other entries are listed, and your resolver on the host fails or takes too long, the requests will be sent using those fallbacks.

It confused me at first too, but @Luap99 mentioned that it will be ignored:

> You can also set nameservers for the entire network with podman network create --dns which works similar. In such cases aardvark-dns should not forward to the resolvers from the resolv.conf in the rootless-netns file.

And indeed I don't see UDP requests to 53 anymore, despite the public servers still being present in the generated `resolv.conf`.
How I hate DNS hahaha. Thanks for the clarification, this would have saved me HOURS!
